title: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias
authors: William H. Sauer, Paul C. Zei

items.subtopic: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias | ATRIOVENTRICULAR NODAL REENTRY TACHYCARDIA
items.title: Mechanism and Pathophysiology of AVNRT
items.content: AV nodal reentry tachycardia (AVNRT) involves a reentrant circuit within the AV node and perinodal atrium, facilitated by dual pathways for conduction. The typical form uses a slow pathway extending from the compact AV node to the coronary sinus floor, with the wavefront moving through this path before exiting via a fast pathway.
keyPoints: AVNRT is the most common PSVT, representing ~60% of cases referred for catheter ablation.
Occurs predominantly in the second to fourth decades, more often in women.
Reentry involves dual pathways: slow (right inferior extension) and fast within the AV node.
P wave may be obscured or appear as pseudo-r' in V1 due to simultaneous atrial and ventricular activation.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias | ATRIOVENTRICULAR NODAL REENTRY TACHYCARDIA

items.subtopic: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias | ATRIOVENTRICULAR NODAL REENTRY TACHYCARDIA
items.title: Clinical Presentation and Diagnosis of AVNRT
items.content: AVNRT is usually well tolerated but can cause angina, pulmonary edema, hypotension, or syncope in rapid cases. The P wave may be difficult to discern due to its timing with the QRS complex, often appearing as pseudo-r' in V1.
keyPoints: Symptoms include palpitations; severe symptoms like angina and syncope can occur, especially in the elderly.
P waves are typically hidden within or immediately after the QRS complex.
Inverted P waves may be seen between QRS complexes in unusual forms.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias | ATRIOVENTRICULAR NODAL REENTRY TACHYCARDIA

items.subtopic: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias | ATRIOVENTRICULAR NODAL REENTRY TACHYCARDIA
items.title: Management and Treatment of AVNRT
items.content: Acute management involves vagal maneuvers or medications like beta blockers, verapamil, or diltiazem. For recurrent or severe cases, catheter ablation is recommended, which is curative in >95% of patients with a <1% risk of requiring permanent pacemaker implantation.
keyPoints: Vagal maneuvers and medications (beta blockers, verapamil, diltiazem) can terminate acute episodes.
Chronic therapy options include beta blockers or flecainide for prophylaxis.
Catheter ablation is curative in >95% of patients; major risk is AV block requiring pacemaker implantation (<1%).
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias | ATRIOVENTRICULAR NODAL REENTRY TACHYCARDIA

items.subtopic: JUNCTIONAL TACHYCARDIA | ACCESSORY PATHWAYS AND THE WOLFF-PARKINSON-WHITE SYNDROME
items.title: Diagnosing Junctional Ectopic Tachycardia (JET)
items.content: Junctional ectopic tachycardia is characterized by automaticity within the AV node, often presenting as a narrow QRS tachycardia with ventriculoatrial block and AV dissociation. It frequently occurs in children post-surgery for congenital heart disease or after increased adrenergic tone, such as following isoproterenol administration.
keyPoints: JET presents as a narrow QRS tachycardia with ventriculoatrial block and AV dissociation.
Common in children post-surgery for congenital heart disease.
May occur after increased adrenergic tone or isoproterenol administration.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · JUNCTIONAL TACHYCARDIA | ACCESSORY PATHWAYS AND THE WOLFF-PARKINSON-WHITE SYNDROME

items.subtopic: JUNCTIONAL TACHYCARDIA | ACCESSORY PATHWAYS AND THE WOLFF-PARKINSON-WHITE SYNDROME
items.title: Understanding Accessory Pathways and WPW Syndrome
items.content: Accessory pathways (APs) are abnormal atrioventricular connections present from birth, leading to preexcitation during sinus rhythm. Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome is defined by a preexcited QRS in sinus rhythm and episodes of paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia.
keyPoints: APs occur in 1 in 1500–2000 people, often with structurally normal hearts.
Preexcitation during sinus rhythm shows a short P-R interval (<0.12 s) and delta wave on ECG.
WPW syndrome involves preexcited QRS and episodes of PSVT.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · JUNCTIONAL TACHYCARDIA | ACCESSORY PATHWAYS AND THE WOLFF-PARKINSON-WHITE SYNDROME

items.subtopic: JUNCTIONAL TACHYCARDIA | ACCESSORY PATHWAYS AND THE WOLFF-PARKINSON-WHITE SYNDROME
items.title: ECG Characteristics of Accessory Pathways
items.content: The ECG characteristics of accessory pathways depend on their location, affecting the morphology of the QRS and delta wave. Right-sided pathways produce a left bundle branch block-like configuration in lead V1, while left-sided pathways may mimic lateral wall infarction.
keyPoints: Right-sided APs create a left bundle branch block-like pattern in V1.
Left-sided APs can mimic Q waves of lateral wall infarction with a negative delta wave in aVL.
Delta waves are determined by the AP location and site of earliest ventricular activation.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · JUNCTIONAL TACHYCARDIA | ACCESSORY PATHWAYS AND THE WOLFF-PARKINSON-WHITE SYNDROME

items.subtopic: ATRIOVENTRICULAR RECIPROCATING TACHYCARDIA
items.title: Mechanism and Termination of Orthodromic AV Reciprocating Tachycardia
items.content: Orthodromic AV reciprocating tachycardia involves a reentrant circuit where the wavefront travels anterogradely over the AV node to the ventricles, then retrogradely via an accessory pathway (AP) back to the atrium. Termination occurs with either AV or VA block.
keyPoints: Reentry wavefront propagates from atria to ventricles and back through AP.
QRS is narrow or shows typical bundle branch block without preexcitation during tachycardia.
AV or VA block terminates the tachycardia.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · ATRIOVENTRICULAR RECIPROCATING TACHYCARDIA

items.subtopic: ATRIOVENTRICULAR RECIPROCATING TACHYCARDIA
items.title: Distinguishing Features and Clinical Implications of Orthodromic AV Reciprocating Tachycardia
items.content: In orthodromic AV reciprocating tachycardia, P waves follow the QRS complex due to retrograde conduction over the AP. The morphology varies with pathway location; posteroseptal pathways show negative P waves in leads II, III, and aVE. Slow retrograde conduction can lead to permanent junctional reciprocating tachycardia (PJRT) and potential tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy.
keyPoints: P waves follow QRS due to retrograde AP conduction.
Posteroseptal APs show negative P waves in leads II, III, and aVE.
Slow retrograde conduction can cause PJRT and tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · ATRIOVENTRICULAR RECIPROCATING TACHYCARDIA

items.subtopic: PREEXCITED TACHYCARDIAS
items.title: Why is AV nodal-blocking therapy contraindicated in preexcited AF?
items.content: In patients with preexcited atrial fibrillation (AF), the presence of an accessory pathway (AP) that allows rapid ventricular conduction can lead to life-threatening arrhythmias. Administering AV nodal-blocking agents, such as verapamil, diltiazem, beta blockers, adenosine, or amiodarone, can slow AV nodal conduction without affecting the AP, thereby facilitating and accelerating antegrade conduction over the AP. This increases the risk of ventricular fibrillation and sudden death.
keyPoints: Preexcited AF presents as a wide-complex, very irregular rhythm.
Approximately 25% of APs allow R-to-R intervals <250 ms during AF, increasing the risk of ventricular fibrillation and sudden death.
AV nodal-blocking agents are contraindicated in preexcited AF due to the potential for accelerating ventricular rate via the AP.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · PREEXCITED TACHYCARDIAS

items.subtopic: PREEXCITED TACHYCARDIAS
items.title: How should rapid preexcited tachycardia be managed?
items.content: Rapid preexcited tachycardias, such as antidromic AV reciprocating tachycardia or preexcited AF, require immediate intervention due to the risk of ventricular fibrillation and sudden death. Treatment options include electrical cardioversion for rapid termination of the arrhythmia or intravenous administration of procainamide or ibutilide to terminate the arrhythmia or slow the ventricular rate.
keyPoints: Rapid preexcited tachycardias should be treated with electrical cardioversion.
Intravenous procainamide or ibutilide can be used to terminate the arrhythmia or slow the ventricular rate.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · PREEXCITED TACHYCARDIAS

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF PATIENTS WITH ACCESSORY PATHWAYS
items.title: Indications for Electrophysiology Study and Catheter Ablation in WPW Syndrome
items.content: Patients with Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome who experience symptoms of arrhythmia are at risk for atrial fibrillation (AF) and sudden death due to rapid antegrade conduction through the accessory pathway (AP). An invasive electrophysiology study is recommended to assess the potential for dangerously rapid heart rates if AF occurs. Catheter ablation, with an efficacy rate of 95% depending on AP location, is advised for recurrent arrhythmias when drugs are ineffective or not tolerated.
keyPoints: Risk of cardiac arrest in adults: ~2 per 1000 patients; higher in children
Electrophysiology study recommended to assess risk of rapid antegrade conduction
Catheter ablation efficacy: ~95% depending on AP location
Serious complications from catheter ablation occur in <3%
Procedure mortality is <1 per 1000 patients
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENTS WITH ACCESSORY PATHWAYS

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF PATIENTS WITH ACCESSORY PATHWAYS
items.title: Risk Assessment and Management for Asymptomatic Preexcitation
items.content: Asymptomatic adults with preexcitation have a low risk of sudden death, estimated at 1 per 1000 patient-years. Electrophysiology studies are advised for those in high-risk occupations or who desire evaluation. Routine follow-up without therapy is reasonable for others. Children have a higher risk of sudden death, ~2 per 1000 patient-years.
keyPoints: Risk of sudden death in asymptomatic adults: 1 per 1000 patient-years
Electrophysiology study advised for high-risk occupations (e.g., police, military, pilots)
Routine follow-up without therapy is reasonable for low-risk individuals
Children have a higher risk of sudden death: ~2 per 1000 patient-years
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENTS WITH ACCESSORY PATHWAYS

items.subtopic: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardia
items.title: Management of Acute PSVT in Stable Patients
items.content: In stable patients with paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (PSVT), initial management involves vagal maneuvers and pharmacological agents targeting AV nodal conduction. Continuous ECG monitoring is essential to observe the response, which aids in diagnosis.
keyPoints: Most PSVTs are dependent on AV nodal conduction (AV nodal reentry or orthodromic AV reentry).
Vagal maneuvers include carotid sinus massage and Valsalva maneuver.
Carotid sinus massage should be avoided if there is a history of stroke or presence of carotid bruits.
Continuous ECG monitoring during treatment helps establish the diagnosis.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardia

items.subtopic: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardia
items.title: Indications for Cardioversion in PSVT
items.content: In cases where PSVT presents with severe symptoms such as hypotension, unconsciousness, or respiratory distress, immediate QRS-synchronous direct current cardioversion is indicated. However, intravenous adenosine is typically effective and preferred due to its prompt action.
keyPoints: QRS-synchronous direct current cardioversion is warranted in the presence of hypotension with unconsciousness or respiratory distress.
Intravenous adenosine works promptly in most situations and is usually sufficient.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardia

items.subtopic: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias | ATRIOVENTRICULAR NODAL REENTRY TACHYCARDIA
items.question: Which pathway is most commonly utilized in typical forms of AV nodal reentry tachycardia (AVNRT)?
items.explanation: Most forms of AVNRT utilize a slowly conducting AV nodal pathway (right inferior extension).
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias | ATRIOVENTRICULAR NODAL REENTRY TACHYCARDIA

items.subtopic: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias | ATRIOVENTRICULAR NODAL REENTRY TACHYCARDIA
items.question: What is the most common manifestation of AVNRT in terms of patient demographics?
options: Most common in children under 10 years
More often occurs in women during their second to fourth decades of life
items.explanation: AVNRT most commonly manifests in the second to fourth decades of life, more often in women.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias | ATRIOVENTRICULAR NODAL REENTRY TACHYCARDIA

items.subtopic: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias | ATRIOVENTRICULAR NODAL REENTRY TACHYCARDIA
items.question: What is a characteristic feature of the P wave during typical AVNRT?
options: P wave occurs before QRS and is easily discernible
P wave is inscribed during, slightly before, or slightly after the QRS
P wave appears as a large positive deflection in lead V1
P wave is absent due to simultaneous atrial and ventricular contraction
items.explanation: The P wave is inscribed during, slightly before, or slightly after the QRS.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias | ATRIOVENTRICULAR NODAL REENTRY TACHYCARDIA

items.subtopic: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias | ATRIOVENTRICULAR NODAL REENTRY TACHYCARDIA
items.question: What is a potential acute treatment for AVNRT?
options: Administration of an oral beta blocker or verapamil at the onset of an episode
Use of calcium channel blockers only after episodes have ceased
items.explanation: Acute treatment can include administration of an oral beta blocker, verapamil, or diltiazem at the onset of an episode.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias | ATRIOVENTRICULAR NODAL REENTRY TACHYCARDIA

items.subtopic: JUNCTIONAL TACHYCARDIA | ACCESSORY PATHWAYS AND THE WOLFF-PARKINSON-WHITE SYNDROME
items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature of functional ectopic tachycardia (JET) in children?
options: It presents as a wide QRS tachycardia.
It often occurs with ventriculoatrial block and AV dissociation.
It is commonly triggered by beta-blocker administration.
items.explanation: JET presents as a narrow QRS tachycardia, often with ventriculoatrial (VA) block and AV dissociation.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · JUNCTIONAL TACHYCARDIA | ACCESSORY PATHWAYS AND THE WOLFF-PARKINSON-WHITE SYNDROME

items.subtopic: JUNCTIONAL TACHYCARDIA | ACCESSORY PATHWAYS AND THE WOLFF-PARKINSON-WHITE SYNDROME
items.question: What is the typical QRS duration in Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome during sinus rhythm?
options: Shortened due to a short P-R interval.
Prolonged due to slow conduction through direct activation of ventricular myocardium over the AP.
Normal, as there is no preexcitation.
Variable and dependent on heart rate.
items.explanation: In WPW syndrome, the QRS duration is prolonged due to slow conduction through direct activation of ventricular myocardium over the accessory pathway (AP).
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · JUNCTIONAL TACHYCARDIA | ACCESSORY PATHWAYS AND THE WOLFF-PARKINSON-WHITE SYNDROME

items.subtopic: JUNCTIONAL TACHYCARDIA | ACCESSORY PATHWAYS AND THE WOLFF-PARKINSON-WHITE SYNDROME
items.question: Which location is most frequently associated with accessory pathways in Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome?
options: Between the right atrium and free wall of the left ventricle.
Between the left atrium and free wall of the left ventricle.
items.explanation: Accessory pathways most frequently occur between the left atrium and free wall of the left ventricle in Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · JUNCTIONAL TACHYCARDIA | ACCESSORY PATHWAYS AND THE WOLFF-PARKINSON-WHITE SYNDROME

items.subtopic: JUNCTIONAL TACHYCARDIA | ACCESSORY PATHWAYS AND THE WOLFF-PARKINSON-WHITE SYNDROME
items.question: What is a potential consequence of right-sided accessory pathways?
options: They produce a right bundle branch block-like configuration in lead V1.
They often create marked preexcitation due to proximity to the sinus node.
They result in a negative delta wave in aVL.
They are associated with minimal or absent preexcitation on 12-lead ECG.
items.explanation: Right-sided pathways produce a left bundle branch block-like configuration in lead V1 and often create marked preexcitation due to their proximity to the sinus node.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · JUNCTIONAL TACHYCARDIA | ACCESSORY PATHWAYS AND THE WOLFF-PARKINSON-WHITE SYNDROME

items.subtopic: ATRIOVENTRICULAR RECIPROCATING TACHYCARDIA
items.question: What is the typical QRS complex appearance during orthodromic AV reciprocating tachycardia?
options: Narrow or with typical right or left bundle branch block, but without preexcitation
items.explanation: The QRS is typically narrow or may have a right or left bundle branch block, but without preexcitation during tachycardia.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · ATRIOVENTRICULAR RECIPROCATING TACHYCARDIA

items.subtopic: ATRIOVENTRICULAR RECIPROCATING TACHYCARDIA
items.question: What happens to the P waves in relation to the QRS complex during orthodromic AV reciprocating tachycardia?
options: P waves are simultaneous with a narrow QRS complex
P waves precede the QRS complex
P waves follow the QRS complex
items.explanation: During orthodromic AV reciprocating tachycardia, P waves always follow the QRS and are never simultaneous with a narrow QRS complex.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · ATRIOVENTRICULAR RECIPROCATING TACHYCARDIA

items.subtopic: ATRIOVENTRICULAR RECIPROCATING TACHYCARDIA
items.question: Which condition can result from slow retrograde conduction over an accessory pathway (AP) during tachycardia?
items.explanation: Slow retrograde conduction over an AP can lead to permanent junctional reciprocating tachycardia (PJRT).
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · ATRIOVENTRICULAR RECIPROCATING TACHYCARDIA

items.subtopic: PREEXCITED TACHYCARDIAS
items.question: Which mechanism is most commonly associated with antidromic AV reciprocating tachycardia in preexcited tachycardias?
options: Activation propagates from atrium to ventricle via the His-Purkinje system and retrogradely to the atria via the AP.
Activation propagates from atrium to ventricle via the AP and then conducts retrogradely to the atria via the His-Purkinje system.
Conduction occurs solely through the AV node without involving the AP.
Activation is entirely due to rapid conduction over specialized His-Purkinje fibers.
items.explanation: Antidromic AV reciprocating tachycardia involves antegrade conduction from atrium to ventricle via the AP and retrograde conduction to the atria via the His-Purkinje system.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · PREEXCITED TACHYCARDIAS

items.subtopic: PREEXCITED TACHYCARDIAS
items.question: What is a potential life-threatening risk associated with preexcited atrial fibrillation (AF) or atrial flutter?
options: Bradycardia due to AV nodal conduction slowing.
Ventricular fibrillation and sudden death due to rapid repetitive conduction over the AP.
Complete heart block resulting from excessive AV nodal blocking agents.
Stable ventricular rate maintained by normal AV node conduction.
items.explanation: Preexcited AF or atrial flutter can be life-threatening if rapid repetitive conduction over the AP leads to ventricular fibrillation and sudden death.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · PREEXCITED TACHYCARDIAS

items.subtopic: PREEXCITED TACHYCARDIAS
items.question: Which treatment is contraindicated during preexcited atrial fibrillation?
items.explanation: AV nodal-blocking agents, including oral or intravenous verapamil, are contraindicated during preexcited AF as they can facilitate AP conduction and dangerously accelerate the ventricular rate.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · PREEXCITED TACHYCARDIAS

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF PATIENTS WITH ACCESSORY PATHWAYS
items.question: Which diagnostic procedure is recommended to assess whether an accessory pathway (AP) can support dangerously rapid heart rates if atrial fibrillation were to occur?
items.explanation: An invasive electrophysiology study is recommended for assessing the risk of rapid heart rates through an AP.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENTS WITH ACCESSORY PATHWAYS

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF PATIENTS WITH ACCESSORY PATHWAYS
items.question: What is a potential serious complication of catheter ablation in patients with accessory pathways?
items.explanation: Serious complications from catheter ablation can include AV block.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENTS WITH ACCESSORY PATHWAYS

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF PATIENTS WITH ACCESSORY PATHWAYS
items.question: What is the estimated risk of sudden death in adults with preexcitation but no arrhythmia symptoms?
items.explanation: The risk of sudden death in asymptomatic adults with preexcitation is estimated to be 1 per 1000 patient-years.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENTS WITH ACCESSORY PATHWAYS

items.subtopic: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardia
items.question: What is the initial management approach for a stable patient with PSVT?
items.explanation: Stable individuals with PSVT are initially managed using sympathetic and vagotonic maneuvers due to the AV nodal dependency of most PSVTs.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardia

items.subtopic: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardia
items.question: When is QRS-synchronous direct current cardioversion indicated in PSVT management?
options: In all cases of PSVT for rapid conversion
For stable patients with no symptoms
In the presence of hypotension with unconsciousness or respiratory distress
After vagal maneuvers have been attempted
items.explanation: QRS-synchronous direct current cardioversion is warranted in PSVT when there is hypotension with unconsciousness or respiratory distress.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardia

items.subtopic: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardia
items.question: What does transient AV block during vagal maneuvers indicate about the mechanism of tachycardia?
options: AV nodal reentry or orthodromic AV reentry
items.explanation: Transient AV block with only slowing of tachycardia may expose ongoing P waves, indicating AT or atrial flutter as the mechanism.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardia

items.statement: AVNRT is usually associated with structural heart disease.
items.explanation: AVNRT is not usually associated with structural heart disease.

items.statement: Accessory pathways are associated with Ebstein's anomaly of the tricuspid valve.
items.explanation: APs are indeed associated with Ebstein's anomaly of the tricuspid valve.

items.subtopic: JUNCTIONAL TACHYCARDIA | ACCESSORY PATHWAYS AND THE WOLFF-PARKINSON-WHITE SYNDROME
items.statement: Concealed accessory pathways allow antegrade conduction from atrium to ventricle.
items.explanation: Concealed APs allow only retrograde conduction, from ventricle to atrium.

items.statement: During sinus rhythm, preexcitation is observed if the accessory pathway allows anterograde conduction.
items.explanation: Preexcitation during sinus rhythm occurs when the accessory pathway also permits anterograde conduction.

items.statement: The P-R interval is typically longer than the R-P interval during orthodromic AV reciprocating tachycardia.

items.statement: Carotid sinus massage is contraindicated in patients with a history of stroke.
items.explanation: Carotid sinus massage should be avoided if there's a prior history of stroke due to the risk of carotid vascular disease.

items.statement: Intravenous adenosine is rarely needed in acute management of PSVT.

items.assertion: AVNRT can cause significant symptoms despite not being life-threatening.
items.reason: The rapid tachycardia in AVNRT may lead to angina, pulmonary edema, hypotension, or syncope.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why AVNRT can cause significant symptoms.

items.assertion: The P wave in typical AVNRT is easily discernible.
items.reason: Atrial activation occurs at about the same time as ventricular activation, making it difficult to discern.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the P wave can be difficult to discern; however, the reason correctly explains why.

items.assertion: Accelerated junctional rhythm can be related to increased sympathetic tone.

items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.subtopic: JUNCTIONAL TACHYCARDIA | ACCESSORY PATHWAYS AND THE WOLFF-PARKINSON-WHITE SYNDROME
items.assertion: Preexcitation due to an accessory pathway can disappear during exercise.
items.reason: Conduction over the AV node accelerates and may take over ventricular activation completely during exercise.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · JUNCTIONAL TACHYCARDIA | ACCESSORY PATHWAYS AND THE WOLFF-PARKINSON-WHITE SYNDROME

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF PATIENTS WITH ACCESSORY PATHWAYS
items.assertion: The risk of cardiac arrest is higher in children than adults with accessory pathways.
items.reason: Children have a greater estimated risk of sudden death, ~2 per 1000 patient-years compared to adults.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the risk is higher in children.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · MANAGEMENT OF PATIENTS WITH ACCESSORY PATHWAYS

items.subtopic: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardia
items.assertion: A Valsalva maneuver should be attempted in cooperative individuals with PSVT.
items.reason: The Valsalva maneuver can help terminate the tachycardia by increasing vagal tone, which is effective for AV nodal dependent arrhythmias.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why a Valsalva maneuver should be attempted.
items.reference: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardias · Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardia
